The context repeatedly mentions the emission reductions for biomass and SOC, but does not provide the necessary information to calculate the percentage change in SOC stock. CL ID  06  Section no.  3.9  Date: 13/09/2022: The Ex-ante model predicts that by year 15, 89,031 net tCO2 will be sequestered in the project area  compared to 144,652 net tCO2 ex-post. This represents a -38.5% difference. This is not unexpected  as  a  conservative  IPPC  value  for  maximum  biomass  was  used  to  constrain  the  ex-ante  model.  According to the 2006 IPCC NGGI for Forest Lands, biomass in tropical moist deciduous forest in  Africa can range between 160 and 430 tonnes dry matter per hectare. For the ex-ante model a value  of 191 tonnes dry matter per hectare was used, hence this is considered conservative. Therefore, it  is not unexpected the ex-post value is performing better than the ex-ante estimate given a biomass  value towards the lower end of the expected range was used in the ex-ante model.

Technical description of the project 
 
The technology employed in this project is Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration (FMNR), and 
planting of seedlings raised from nursery stock.
The FMNR technique has been developed in Niger 
Republic, West Africa over 20 years where it is now practiced on over 2 million hectares.

from the field team and auditor and time elapsed between when the field team undertook 
measurements and the auditor.

compared to World Vision’s field team, then it’s the PD’s view that carbon stocks have been 
conservatively estimated.

SDG 13 Climate Action 
The project emission reductions calculation is calculated using the CDM methodology 
(AR-AM0003 version 4).
The project uses the Stock Change method, and within the method, tree dimensions 
are converted to tree biomass by applying allometric equations.
The process uses 
equations 60-61 and 68-81.

Actual GHG removals by sinks therefore can be calculated by taking the pre-existing biomass from the 
project, established at validation (31,186 tonnes of CO2e), and subtracting this from the total biomass 
calculated from the permanent sample plots (84,048).
Since there are no project emissions, the total net 
actual GHG removal by sinks is 52,862.
Therefore, the VERs accrued from the project activity in this 
monitoring period is 52,862 tCO2e.

The project owner is World Vision Australia, who is facilitating the sale of the VERs on behalf of the  communities who have secured the user rights from the government. Letters from the communities  endorsing  World  Vision  Australia  to  act  in  this  role  are  attached  in  reference  documents  Legal  confirmation of carbon rights_20.07.2010.pdf and Letter of delegation_20.02.2012.pdf.  The  project  is  being  implemented  on  communal  land  which  in  Ethiopia  is  always  owned  by  the  Federal government. The project developer has facilitated the securing of land user rights from the  Ethiopian government, and this is regarded as the strongest form of land tenure available in Ethiopia.  The user rights certification, and the legislation which enable these rights, clearly articulates that the  community is the owner of the land and the  owner of  the timber. World Vision has secured legal  advice on behalf of the communities which confirms that the granting of user rights also grants the  holder of such rights the ownership of the carbon rights contained within the forest.   A copy of the user rights certificate, which has been secured by the forestry cooperatives, is included  as reference document (refer to folder ‘Sodo user rights’). The legislation which facilitates the transfer  of land user rights is available on request.

Technical description of the project 
 
The technology employed in this project is Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration (FMNR), and 
planting of seedlings raised from nursery stock.
The FMNR technique has been developed in Niger 
Republic, West Africa over 20 years where it is now practiced on over 2 million hectares.
The technique

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence from the GS-AR_Soddo_CertificationReport(1).pdf, Page: 17, mentions that all World Vision staffs are eligible to 100% medical coverage up to 100,000 ETB/year (around 5000 USD) to all its employees and families, and have 24 hours insurance coverage for accidents. However, this information does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Total Number of employees paid living wage'. The Anker methodology is mentioned for calculation, but no quantitative data is provided to support the indicator. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative and does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Harvest 
 
Selective harvesting will be used across the project area.
The PDD states the following harvesting 
regime will be followed: 
 
• 
Establishment of an average of 1500 stems per hectare over the project area of canopy species at 
the end of year 1 
• 
Thinning to a stocking rate of 600 stems per hectare at year 15 
• 
Selective harvest of 15 stems per hectare each 5 years beginning at year 10.

Life on land.summary: The evidence from the project monitoring reports and certification documents provides some information on sustainable forest management practices, but it is mostly qualitative. The project follows a rotation forestry concept of about 15 to 20 years for plantation areas (Soddo+Carbon+MONITORING+REPORT+4+June+2017, Page: 11), and pruning, thinning, and harvesting are undertaken in accordance with the Forest Management Plan (Soddo+Carbon+MONITORING+REPORT+4+June+2017, Page: 11). The project also employs Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration (FMNR) techniques, which involve singling, pruning, and thinning in naturally regenerating forests (Soddo+Carbon+MONITORING+REPORT+4+June+2017, Page: 10). However, there is no explicit quantitative data on the percentage of forest areas under sustainable management practices. Therefore, the evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, and the score is 1.

Regeneration (FMNR) Guideline2.
Activities like singling, pruning and thinning in the beginning of a naturally regenerating forest (which 
has been degraded the years before) are no separate management activities.
A decision of management implications is taken for each stump.
The tallest and straightest stems are 
selected and the remaining stems culled/removed.

15. Life on land.indicator: Total above and below ground biomass stock in forest

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided directly measures the indicator variable 'Total area of Trees Planted' with a specific quantitative value of 32.57 hectares, as stated in the Certification Report (Document: GS-AR_Soddo_CertificationReport, Page: 14). This evidence satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement. However, the evidence does not provide a comprehensive picture of the total area of land on which trees were planted, as it only mentions a specific area of 32.57 hectares, whereas the afforestation and reforestation activity covers approximately 503 hectares of land across 1 stratum (Document: 231104+GS3007_Sodo_Monitoring-Report_RP2_v8, Page: 4).

planted trees.
To prepare the planting area, weeds are generally controlled by mechanical means.
Because of possible 
chemical hazards, herbicides are not utilized for weeding.
Trees will be planted in rows, at a 1.5 x 1.5 m / 2 x 2 m distance resulting in a total number of 4444 / 
2500 planted trees per hectare.
The saplings must have a minimum size of at least 15 cm at the time of 
planting, to be able to out-compete surrounding vegetation.

SECTION A. DESCRIPTION OF PROJECT 
A.1.
General description of project  
 
The afforestation and reforestation activity of the ‘Sodo Ethiopia’ Project (the Project), 
involves the restoration of indigenous tree species on Mt. Damota in a mountainous

The boundaries surrounding the planting areas

The marking of the planting lines is done by using ropes according to a triangle arrangement of the trees 
in 1.5 x 1.5 / 2 x 2 m spacing.
In hilly terrain, the trees are planted on their contour line.
Prior to 
planting, the location of each tree is determined according to the species and site requirements and the 
future planting hole is then marked with a clearly visible stick that will jut out of the ground at least 30 
cm.

Technical description of the project 
 
The technology employed in this project is Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration (FMNR), and 
planting of seedlings raised from nursery stock.
The FMNR technique has been developed in Niger 
Republic, West Africa over 20 years where it is now practiced on over 2 million hectares.
The technique

species and growth areas with different potential of growth.

area; planting of seedlings that are produced in nursery remains necessary.
Thus, artificial plantation 
will be used depending on the extent of forest degradation and open gaps left between trees.

2.2.6 Planting 
 
The planting period should start early enough to allow the newly planted trees to receive at least 3 
months of rains in order to ensure proper growth through sufficient water supply in line with normal 
climate patterns.
Therefore, the planting activities take place in the rainy season between June and 
September.
Planting is done in the early morning to reduce sun-related stress on saplings and people.
Whenever 
possible, planting is done on cloudy days.

The evidence from the GS-AR_Soddo_CertificationReport%281%29.pdf document on Page 4 states that the total land area of the project that has been designated as protected is 503.4 hectares. This evidence directly measures the indicator variable, but it does not meet the indicator threshold of 10% of the project area being protected. Therefore, the evidence partially satisfies the measurement requirement.

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Total Area of Trees Planted: Native Species'. Although the context mentions that 32.57 ha of plantation area was used with Grevillea and Cupressus tree species, and supplemental planting was used to augment the FMNR established forests with 32.6 hectares planted, the total land area where native species were planted is not explicitly stated. The numbers mentioned do not measure the indicator variable directly, so the evidence is treated as qualitative (Score = 1). However, the evidence does not contain explicit quantitative data that directly measures or clearly supports the indicator, so the final score is 1.

2.2.2 Plantation establishment 
 
Plantations are established on areas where there is no secondary forest, or on bare land formerly used as 
cattle grazing areas but have become degraded.
2.2.3 Species Selection

involves the restoration of indigenous tree species on Mt. Damota in a mountainous region of South 
Western Ethiopia.
The project zone covers approximately 503 hectares of land and includes 1 strata.
The project contributes to climate change mitigation objectives by creating Greenhouse gas (GHG) 
sinks through assisted natural regeneration of degraded lands.
Furthermore, the project compliments the 
natural resource management goals of the Ethiopian Agricultural Rural Development and Forestry

Supplemental planting 
 
Supplemental planting was used to augment the FMNR established forests.
Three nurseries were 
established with capacity to deliver up to 700,000 seedlings per year for the first four years, sufficient to 
reforest 500 hectares at 1000 stems per hectare.
However during project implementation only 32.6 
hectares has been planted.
The nursery was established in a conventional manner.

The Project has established five community cooperative societies, which have legal 
ownership to 503 hectares of community land.

using the Farmer Managed Natural Regeneration techniques for the purposes of

Bylaws agreed upon at project 
inception form the rules for community cooperative societies to manage the project.

Harvest 
 
Selective harvesting will be used across the project area.

regime will be followed: 
 
• 
Establishment of an average of 1500 stems per hectare over the project area of canopy species at 
the end of year 1 
• 
Thinning to a stocking rate of 600 stems per hectare at year 15 
• 
Selective harvest of 15 stems per hectare each 5 years beginning at year 10.

development in the following ways:

Regeneration of native forest, utilizing the farmer managed natural 
regeneration (FMNR) and traditional forest establishment techniques.

Enhancement of GHG removals by sinks in the project area.
3.
Promotion of native vegetation and biodiversity in the project area, which can 
be utilized as a refuge for local and migratory species and to improve the

2.2 Management of Plantation Areas 
In restoring degraded tropical forest like the Soddo Community Managed Natural Regeneration Project 
area; planting of seedlings that are produced in nursery remains necessary.
Thus, artificial plantation 
will be used depending on the extent of forest degradation and open gaps left between trees.

ownership to 503 hectares of community land.

Due to the complexity of the reporting, separate monitoring reports were established in monitoring  period  1  and  continued  in  monitoring  period  2  rather  than  incorporating  them  into  the  GS  MR  template.: 1.  Project  representative  has  clarified  that  due  to  the  complexity  of  the  reporting,  separate  monitoring reports were established in MP1 and has been continues in MP2. However, the  reports for ‘Avifauna  diversity’, ‘Tree species diversity’ and ‘Forestry and Water’  have  not  been provided to the verification team.   2.  Furthermore, project representative shall clarify the mechanism of fire and illegal logging in  the Sustainability Monitoring report.   3.  The household survey sample records shall also be provided to the verification team for the  forest-based  livelihoods  and  quantitative  results  obtained  shall  be  provided  in  the  Sustainability Monitoring report. The selection process of the sampled households and the  employed confidence interval/ precision level shall also be indicated.   4.  The evidence of the capacity building trainings conducted shall be provided and the exact  number of trainings carried out during the monitoring period shall be provided.   5.  For ‘Food Security’, project representative shall provide the quantitative results obtained for  improvement  in  the  fertility  of  soil,  productivity  and  production.  PP  shall  also  clarify  the  process of monitoring such results.   6.  For ‘Health and water security’ PP shall provide  evidence of 30 springs  replenishing and  provide  evidence  how  the  water  from  the  springs  was  confirmed  to  be  a  source  of  clean  water.
